bottom_line: This is the right kind of bet: it buys a capability we'd otherwise spend ~18 months building, into an adjacent buyer we already sell to. Strategically a GO — the disagreement with the CFO is about price, not about whether.

capital_allocation_fit.priority_bucket: Grow the core / Fund new bets — extends what already works into an adjacent segment
capital_allocation_fit.build_vs_buy_vs_partner: BUY beats BUILD (18-month lead) and PARTNER (we'd cede the AI roadmap); this is core enough to own

tree_of_thought_paths.path: Acquire now at a negotiated price
tree_of_thought_paths.downside: integration drag, pay for decelerating growth
tree_of_thought_paths.second_order: signals to market we own RevOps

tree_of_thought_paths.path: Partner / reseller first, option to buy
tree_of_thought_paths.downside: competitor could buy them; we cede AI control
18-month delay, opportunity cost in a fast category

chosen_path: Acquire — but only at a price that survives the CFO bear case; pursue the negotiated-entry path, not buy-at-ask.
the_one_thing_that_kills_it: Integration stalls because the auth/tenancy tech-debt isn't funded on day one, so we can't carry Acme RevOps upmarket and we've paid a platform price for a mid-market asset. This is the question I am most afraid of.

stakeholder_view.customers: cross-sell to our base is the prize; their top customer's change-of-control consent is a real risk to manage
stakeholder_view.team: retaining the co-founder CTO (AI IP) is non-negotiable — structure earn-out + retention
stakeholder_view.board: a markdown-round acquisition of a strategic asset is a defensible board narrative IF price is disciplined

conditions: Retention package for the co-founder CTO and top 3 AI engineers before close
Integration plan with auth/tenancy on the critical path, funded in the deal model
Defer to CFO on the walk-away price; strategy says yes, the number must still work
reasoning_technique: Tree of Thought — generate >=3 paths, score on upside/downside/reversibility/second-order effects
source: ceo-advisor (capital allocation priorities, Go/No-Go decision framework, 'the one thing that kills us', stakeholder priority order, Tree-of-Thought)
